junior.question: Explain the CSS box model. What is the difference between 'content-box' and 'border-box'?
hints: Content, padding, border, margin are the four areas
border-box includes padding and border inside the declared width
junior.answer: The box model defines four areas around every element: content, padding, border, and margin. In content-box (default), width/height apply only to the content area — padding and border add on top. In border-box, width/height include padding and border, making layout arithmetic far simpler and predictable.

junior.question: What is the difference between Flexbox and CSS Grid? When would you choose one over the other?
hints: Flexbox is one-dimensional (row or column), Grid is two-dimensional
Use Flexbox for component alignment, Grid for overall page layout
junior.answer: Flexbox arranges items along a single axis (row or column) and lets them grow, shrink, and align — perfect for nav bars, button groups, or centering. Grid controls both rows and columns simultaneously, enabling complex two-dimensional layouts. Use Flexbox for component-level alignment and Grid for overall page layout where items must align across both axes.

junior.question: Explain closures in JavaScript. Give a practical example of when you would use one.
hints: A closure is a function that retains access to its outer scope after the outer function has returned
Common use cases: data privacy, factory functions, event handlers
junior.answer: A closure is a function that remembers the variables of its enclosing scope even after that outer function has returned. A classic example is a counter factory: a function creates a private count variable and returns increment/decrement functions — only those inner functions can access count, providing data privacy without classes.

junior.question: What is the difference between 'let', 'const', and 'var' in JavaScript?
hints: var is function-scoped and hoisted; let and const are block-scoped
const prevents reassignment but does not make objects immutable
junior.answer: var is function-scoped, hoisted to undefined, and can be re-declared. let is block-scoped, in the temporal dead zone until its declaration, and cannot be re-declared in the same scope. const is block-scoped, requires an initialiser, and forbids reassignment of the binding — but the referenced object can still be mutated.

junior.question: What is event delegation and why is it useful?
hints: Attach a single listener to a parent element instead of many children
Works because DOM events bubble up through ancestors
junior.answer: Event delegation places one listener on a parent element and uses event.target to identify which child was interacted with, exploiting the fact that DOM events bubble upward. Benefits: fewer listeners in memory, and dynamically added children are automatically covered without re-attaching listeners.

junior.question: What is the Document Object Model (DOM) and how does JavaScript interact with it?
hints: DOM is a tree-structured representation of the HTML document
JavaScript uses APIs like getElementById, querySelector, addEventListener
junior.answer: The DOM is a live, tree-structured object model of an HTML document built by the browser during parsing. Every HTML tag becomes a node. JavaScript interacts via APIs such as querySelector, textContent, setAttribute, and addEventListener. DOM mutations are immediately reflected in the rendered page.

junior.question: What is the difference between '==' and '===' in JavaScript?
hints: '==' performs type coercion before comparison
'===' compares both value and type without coercion
junior.answer: == coerces operands to the same type before comparing, producing surprises like 0 == false evaluating to true. === compares value and type without coercion, so 0 === false is false. Always use === to avoid implicit coercion bugs.

junior.question: What are semantic HTML elements and why do they matter?
hints: Examples: <header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <footer>
Benefits: accessibility, SEO, readability
junior.answer: Semantic elements — header, nav, main, article, section, footer — convey the meaning of content, not just its appearance. They improve accessibility (screen readers build a navigable outline from them), SEO (search engines understand page structure), and code maintainability (self-documenting markup).

junior.question: What is the difference between 'null' and 'undefined' in JavaScript?
hints: undefined means a variable was declared but not assigned
null is an intentional absence of value, explicitly set
junior.answer: undefined is the automatic default for declared-but-uninitialised variables, missing function parameters, and absent object properties. null is an explicit developer assignment meaning 'intentionally no value here'. typeof undefined is 'undefined'; typeof null is 'object' — a historical JavaScript bug.

junior.question: What is CSS specificity and how is it calculated?
hints: Specificity is a weight assigned to a CSS selector that determines which rule wins when there is a conflict
Inline styles > IDs > classes/attributes/pseudo-classes > elements/pseudo-elements
junior.answer: CSS specificity is a numeric weight determining which rule applies when multiple selectors target the same element. It is calculated as a four-part value: inline styles (1-0-0-0), IDs (0-1-0-0), classes/attributes/pseudo-classes (0-0-1-0), and element/pseudo-element selectors (0-0-0-1). The selector with the highest specificity wins; !important overrides everything and should be used sparingly.

junior.question: What are CSS pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements? Give examples of each.
hints: Pseudo-classes target elements in a specific state, e.g. :hover, :focus, :nth-child
Pseudo-elements style a specific part of an element, e.g. ::before, ::after, ::first-line
junior.answer: Pseudo-classes select elements based on state or position in the DOM — :hover applies styles on mouse-over, :focus on keyboard focus, :nth-child(n) on positional order. Pseudo-elements style a generated or specific part of an element — ::before and ::after insert decorative content, ::first-letter styles the opening character. Pseudo-classes use a single colon; pseudo-elements use two (though older syntax used one).

junior.question: What is responsive design and how do you implement it?
hints: Responsive design makes a layout adapt to different screen sizes and devices
Key tools: media queries, flexible grids, fluid images, viewport meta tag
junior.answer: Responsive design ensures a website looks and works well on any screen size by adapting layout, typography, and images. It is implemented with fluid grids using percentage or fr units, flexible images with max-width: 100%, and CSS media queries that apply different rules at defined breakpoints. The viewport meta tag is essential to prevent mobile browsers from scaling down the page.

junior.question: What are CSS media queries and how do you use them?
hints: Media queries apply CSS rules only when certain conditions about the viewport or device are met
Syntax: @media (max-width: 768px) { ... }
junior.answer: Media queries are CSS at-rules that conditionally apply styles based on device characteristics such as viewport width, height, orientation, or resolution. A common pattern is @media (max-width: 768px) to target mobile screens. They can be combined with logical operators (and, not, or comma for 'or'). Modern practice prefers mobile-first: write default styles for small screens and use min-width queries to progressively enhance for larger ones.

junior.question: What are the different CSS position values and when would you use each?
hints: Values: static, relative, absolute, fixed, sticky
absolute is positioned relative to the nearest positioned ancestor
junior.answer: static is the default — elements flow normally and top/left have no effect. relative offsets an element from its normal position without removing it from flow. absolute removes the element from flow and positions it relative to the nearest non-static ancestor. fixed positions relative to the viewport and stays put on scroll. sticky is a hybrid: scrolls with the page until a threshold, then sticks in place.

junior.question: What is a stacking context in CSS and what creates one?
hints: A stacking context determines the order layers are painted (z-index)
A new stacking context is created by position + z-index, opacity < 1, transform, etc.
junior.answer: A stacking context is an isolated layer in which child elements are stacked and painted relative to each other. z-index only works within and relative to the same stacking context. New stacking contexts are created by a positioned element with z-index other than auto, elements with opacity less than 1, any element with a transform, filter, or will-change property. Understanding this prevents z-index battles where raising the number has no effect.

junior.question: What are CSS custom properties (CSS variables) and what are their advantages?
hints: Declared with -- prefix: --primary-color: #3498db; used with var(--primary-color)
They cascade and can be changed dynamically with JavaScript
junior.answer: CSS custom properties are variables declared with a double-dash prefix (--name: value) on a selector, then consumed with var(--name). Unlike preprocessor variables, they are live in the DOM, cascade like any CSS property, can be scoped to specific elements, and can be read and written by JavaScript. This makes dynamic theming and runtime style changes straightforward without class toggling.

junior.question: What is the difference between margin and padding in CSS?
hints: Margin is the space outside the border; padding is the space inside, between content and border
Margins can collapse between adjacent elements; padding never collapses
junior.answer: Padding is the space between an element's content and its border — it takes the element's background color. Margin is the space outside the border, separating the element from its neighbours. Adjacent vertical margins collapse to the larger of the two values (margin collapsing), while padding never collapses. Padding contributes to the element's clickable and visual area; margin does not.

junior.question: What is a CSS reset and why might you use one?
hints: A CSS reset removes browser default styles to create a consistent baseline
Popular options: Normalize.css (preserves useful defaults) vs Eric Meyer's reset (removes everything)
junior.answer: Browsers ship with their own default stylesheets (user-agent styles) that differ across vendors, causing inconsistencies like varying heading margins or form element appearances. A CSS reset removes or normalises these defaults so you start from a predictable baseline. Normalize.css is a popular choice that preserves sensible defaults while fixing known inconsistencies, as opposed to aggressive resets that zero out everything.

junior.question: What does cross-browser compatibility mean and how do you ensure it?
hints: Different browsers may render the same HTML/CSS/JS differently
Use tools like Can I Use, autoprefixer, feature detection (Modernizr), and progressive enhancement
junior.answer: Cross-browser compatibility means ensuring a website functions correctly across different browsers and versions. You ensure it by checking browser support on caniuse.com, using autoprefixer to add vendor prefixes, testing in real browsers (including older versions via BrowserStack), applying feature detection rather than user-agent sniffing, and adopting progressive enhancement so core functionality works everywhere.

junior.question: What is the difference between innerHTML and textContent in JavaScript?
hints: innerHTML parses and renders HTML tags; textContent treats everything as plain text
Using innerHTML with user-generated content opens XSS vulnerabilities
junior.answer: innerHTML gets or sets the HTML markup inside an element, parsing any tags in the assigned string and inserting real DOM nodes. textContent gets or sets the plain text, treating all content as literal characters — no parsing occurs. Setting innerHTML with user-supplied data is a major XSS vector because scripts can be injected; textContent is safe because it never executes HTML.

junior.question: What is the difference between event.preventDefault() and event.stopPropagation()?
hints: preventDefault stops the browser's default action (e.g., form submit, link navigation)
stopPropagation stops the event from bubbling up to parent elements
junior.answer: preventDefault cancels the browser's built-in action associated with the event — preventing a form from submitting, a link from navigating, or a checkbox from toggling. stopPropagation stops the event from bubbling further up the DOM tree, so parent listeners do not receive it. They can be called independently or together depending on the desired behaviour; neither affects the other.

junior.question: What is an IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression) and why was it used?
hints: An IIFE is a function that executes immediately after it is defined
Used to create a private scope before ES6 modules existed
junior.answer: An IIFE is a function expression wrapped in parentheses and immediately called: (function(){ ... })(). It creates a new scope, keeping variables private and avoiding pollution of the global scope. Before ES6 modules, IIFEs were the standard pattern for encapsulating library code. Modern codebases use ES modules instead, but IIFEs still appear in legacy code and compiled bundles.

junior.question: What is the JavaScript prototype chain and how does inheritance work through it?
hints: Every object has an internal [[Prototype]] link to another object (or null)
Property lookup walks up the chain until the property is found or null is reached
junior.answer: Every JavaScript object has an internal [[Prototype]] reference pointing to another object. When a property is accessed, the engine looks on the object itself first; if not found, it follows the prototype link and searches there, continuing up the chain until it reaches null. This chain forms the basis of prototypal inheritance. Classes in ES6+ are syntactic sugar over this mechanism — methods defined on a class prototype are shared across all instances.

junior.question: How does the 'this' keyword work in JavaScript?
hints: 'this' refers to the object that is the current execution context
In arrow functions, 'this' is lexically inherited from the enclosing scope
junior.answer: this refers to the execution context of a function call. In a method call (obj.method()), this is the owning object. In a plain function call in strict mode, this is undefined; in sloppy mode, it is the global object. With call, apply, or bind, this is explicitly set. Arrow functions do not have their own this — they capture it lexically from the surrounding scope at definition time, making them predictable in callbacks.

junior.question: What is the difference between arrow functions and regular functions in JavaScript?
hints: Arrow functions have no own 'this', 'arguments', or 'prototype'
Arrow functions cannot be used as constructors
junior.answer: Arrow functions are concise and inherit this, arguments, and super lexically from the enclosing scope. They cannot be used as constructors (no new), have no prototype property, and cannot be generators. Regular functions have their own this bound at call time, their own arguments object, and can be constructors. Use arrow functions for callbacks and methods that need lexical this; regular functions when you need a dynamic this or a constructor.

junior.question: What is destructuring in JavaScript? Give examples with arrays and objects.
hints: Destructuring extracts values from arrays or objects into distinct variables
Array destructuring uses position; object destructuring uses property names
junior.answer: Destructuring is syntax that unpacks values from arrays or properties from objects into variables. Array destructuring uses position: const [a, b] = [1, 2]. Object destructuring uses property names: const { name, age } = person. You can rename during destructuring ({ name: fullName }), provide defaults ({ age = 18 }), and use rest syntax to collect remaining items. It is commonly used in function parameters to accept option objects cleanly.

junior.question: What are the spread and rest operators in JavaScript?
hints: Both use '...' syntax — spread expands an iterable, rest collects remaining values
Spread is used in function calls, array/object literals; rest is used in parameter lists
junior.answer: The spread operator (...) expands an iterable (array, string) or object properties into individual elements — useful for copying arrays, merging objects, and passing array items as function arguments. The rest operator also uses ... but in function parameters or destructuring patterns to collect remaining values into an array. They share syntax but serve opposite purposes: spread expands; rest collects.

junior.question: What are template literals in JavaScript and what advantages do they offer?
hints: Template literals use backticks and allow embedded expressions with ${expression}
They support multi-line strings natively without escape characters
junior.answer: Template literals are string literals delimited by backticks rather than quotes. They support embedded expressions via ${expression}, which are evaluated and interpolated. They allow multi-line strings without \n escape sequences. Tagged template literals pass the string parts and expression values to a function, enabling use cases like safe SQL escaping, styled-components CSS-in-JS, and i18n.

junior.question: What are the array methods map, filter, and reduce? When would you use each?
hints: map transforms each element and returns a new array of the same length
filter returns a subset; reduce accumulates values into a single result
junior.answer: map applies a function to every element and returns a new array of equal length — use it to transform data. filter passes each element through a predicate and returns only those that pass — use it to subset data. reduce folds all elements into a single accumulated value using a callback and an initial value — use it to sum, flatten, or build an object from an array. All three return new values without mutating the original array.

junior.question: What are ES modules and how do they differ from CommonJS modules?
hints: ES modules use import/export syntax and are statically analysable
CommonJS uses require/module.exports and is dynamic (resolved at runtime)
junior.answer: ES modules (ESM) use import and export statements, are statically analysable at parse time, and support tree shaking. They are the native browser and modern Node.js module system. CommonJS uses require() and module.exports; require calls are dynamic and can appear anywhere, making static analysis difficult. ESM imports are live bindings (changes in the exporting module are reflected); CommonJS exports are copied values.

junior.question: How does the Fetch API work? How do you handle errors with it?
hints: fetch() returns a Promise that resolves with a Response object
A 4xx or 5xx status does NOT reject the promise — check response.ok
junior.answer: fetch(url) returns a Promise that resolves to a Response when the network request completes. To read the body, call response.json() or response.text(), which also return Promises. Crucially, fetch only rejects on network failure — HTTP error statuses (400, 500) still resolve. Always check response.ok or response.status and throw manually if needed. Use try/catch with async/await or a .catch() handler for network errors.

junior.question: What is the difference between localStorage, sessionStorage, and cookies?
hints: localStorage persists until explicitly cleared; sessionStorage clears when the tab closes
Cookies are sent with every HTTP request and can be read by the server
junior.answer: localStorage stores key-value pairs persistently in the browser with no expiry — data survives tab and browser restarts. sessionStorage stores data only for the duration of the page session — cleared when the tab closes. Cookies have an expiry date, a size limit of ~4 KB, and are automatically included in every HTTP request to the matching domain, making them the right choice for server-readable auth tokens. localStorage and sessionStorage are never sent to the server automatically.

junior.question: What is the difference between SVG and raster image formats like PNG?
hints: SVG is vector-based (XML markup), scales without quality loss
PNG/JPEG are raster-based (pixel grids), degrade when scaled up
junior.answer: SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) is an XML-based format describing shapes mathematically — it scales to any size without quality loss and can be styled and animated with CSS or JavaScript. Raster formats like PNG store pixel data in a grid; scaling up causes blurriness. SVG is ideal for logos, icons, and illustrations; PNG suits photographs or images with complex pixel detail where vector description is impractical.

junior.question: What is browser caching and what are the main mechanisms?
hints: Caching stores responses so repeat requests can be served without re-downloading
Key headers: Cache-Control, ETag, Last-Modified, Expires
junior.answer: Browser caching stores copies of resources (HTML, CSS, JS, images) so subsequent visits or requests can be served from the local cache, reducing network round-trips. Cache-Control: max-age sets how long a resource is considered fresh. ETag and Last-Modified enable conditional requests — the browser sends the cached version's identifier; the server responds 304 Not Modified if unchanged. Service Workers can provide fine-grained programmatic caching on top of these HTTP mechanisms.

junior.question: What is the alt attribute on images and why is it important?
hints: alt provides a text alternative for images when they cannot be displayed
Required for screen reader accessibility and improves SEO
junior.answer: The alt attribute provides a textual description of an image for cases where the image cannot be displayed (broken URL, slow connection) and for screen readers used by visually impaired users. Decorative images should have an empty alt='' so screen readers skip them. Meaningful images need descriptive alt text that conveys the image's purpose. Search engines also index alt text, contributing to image SEO.

junior.question: What is the viewport meta tag and why is it necessary for mobile devices?
hints: <meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1'>
Without it, mobile browsers render the page at desktop width and scale it down
junior.answer: The viewport meta tag instructs mobile browsers how to scale and size the viewport. Without it, most mobile browsers render pages at a fixed desktop width (typically 980 px) and then scale them down, making text tiny and layouts broken. Setting width=device-width tells the browser to use the actual device width, and initial-scale=1 prevents default zoom. It is a required element in any responsive design.

junior.question: What are HTML data attributes and how do you access them in JavaScript?
hints: Data attributes store custom data on elements: data-user-id='42'
Accessed via element.dataset.userId in JavaScript
junior.answer: Data attributes allow arbitrary custom data to be embedded directly in HTML elements using the data- prefix (e.g., data-product-id='5'). They do not affect rendering and provide a clean way to associate metadata with elements without hidden inputs or JavaScript-only structures. In JavaScript, they are accessed through the element.dataset property — data-user-id becomes dataset.userId (camelCased). They can also be targeted in CSS with attribute selectors.

junior.question: What is ARIA and why is it important for accessibility?
hints: ARIA stands for Accessible Rich Internet Applications
ARIA attributes provide semantic meaning to dynamic and custom UI components for assistive technologies
junior.answer: ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) is a set of HTML attributes (role, aria-label, aria-expanded, aria-live, etc.) that communicate the purpose, state, and behaviour of UI elements to assistive technologies like screen readers. Native HTML elements have implicit ARIA roles (a button is role=button), but custom components built with divs or spans need explicit ARIA to be understandable. The first rule of ARIA is to use native HTML where possible — ARIA supplements, not replaces.

junior.question: What are CSS preprocessors like SASS and what problems do they solve?
hints: SASS adds variables, nesting, mixins, and partials to CSS
Preprocessors compile to standard CSS before delivery to the browser
junior.answer: CSS preprocessors extend CSS with programming constructs: variables (before native custom properties were widespread), nesting to reflect HTML structure, mixins for reusable style blocks, functions, and partials for splitting large stylesheets into manageable files. SASS (and its SCSS syntax) compiles to standard CSS. They solve maintainability and repetition issues in large stylesheets, though native CSS custom properties and cascade layers now cover some of their use cases.

junior.question: What is npm and what is the purpose of package.json?
hints: npm is Node's package manager for installing JavaScript libraries and tools
package.json records project metadata, dependencies, devDependencies, and scripts
junior.answer: npm (Node Package Manager) is the default package manager for Node.js, used to install, update, and manage JavaScript libraries. package.json is the project manifest: it lists dependencies (needed at runtime), devDependencies (needed only during development/build), the project name and version, and scripts (shortcuts to run build, test, lint commands). package-lock.json pins exact resolved versions for reproducible installs.

junior.question: What is TypeScript and what are its main benefits over plain JavaScript?
hints: TypeScript is a statically typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to JS
Benefits: compile-time type errors, better IDE autocomplete, safer refactoring
junior.answer: TypeScript adds optional static typing to JavaScript. Type annotations are checked at compile time, catching type errors (calling undefined, missing properties) before the code runs. IDEs use type information for autocomplete, inline docs, and safe refactoring. TypeScript is a superset — all valid JavaScript is valid TypeScript, so it can be adopted incrementally. It compiles away at build time; browsers receive plain JavaScript.

junior.question: What is a Single Page Application (SPA) and how does it differ from a traditional multi-page application?
hints: An SPA loads a single HTML page and dynamically updates content via JavaScript
Multi-page apps request a full HTML page from the server on every navigation
junior.answer: An SPA loads one HTML document and dynamically renders views in the browser using JavaScript, intercepting navigation with a client-side router and fetching data via APIs without full page reloads. This gives a fast, app-like feel after the initial load. Multi-page applications (MPAs) serve a full HTML response for every page navigation from the server. SPAs can suffer from slower initial load and poorer default SEO; MPAs are simpler and naturally crawlable.

junior.question: What is the purpose of the <head> element in HTML?
hints: The <head> contains metadata about the document, not visible content
It includes title, meta tags, link to stylesheets, and script tags
junior.answer: The <head> element is the document's metadata container. It holds the page title (<title>), character encoding declaration (<meta charset>), viewport settings, Open Graph meta tags for social sharing, links to external stylesheets (<link rel='stylesheet'>), and optionally script tags for analytics or preloads. Its contents are not rendered in the visible page body but are essential for browser behaviour, SEO, and performance.

junior.question: What are the differences between block-level and inline elements in HTML?
hints: Block elements start on a new line and take full available width by default
Inline elements flow within text and only take up as much width as their content
junior.answer: Block-level elements (div, p, h1-h6, section) start on a new line and stretch to fill their container's full width by default. They can contain both block and inline elements. Inline elements (span, a, strong, em) flow within the text stream and only occupy the space their content requires. You cannot set width/height on inline elements without changing their display value. The distinction can be overridden with the CSS display property.

junior.question: What is the difference between a class and an ID selector in CSS?
hints: An ID must be unique per page; a class can be reused on multiple elements
ID has higher specificity than class
junior.answer: An ID selector (#name) targets a single element that must have a unique id attribute on the page. A class selector (.name) can be applied to any number of elements. Specificity-wise, an ID (0-1-0-0) outweighs any number of class selectors (0-0-1-0), making ID styles hard to override. Best practice is to avoid IDs for styling and reserve them for JavaScript targeting or fragment navigation.

junior.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous JavaScript?
hints: Synchronous code executes line by line, blocking further execution until complete
Asynchronous code (callbacks, Promises, async/await) allows other work while waiting
junior.answer: Synchronous JavaScript executes one statement at a time on the single thread; each line must complete before the next begins. Long synchronous operations (like a huge loop or a blocking network request) freeze the UI. Asynchronous operations — file reads, HTTP requests, timers — are offloaded and their completion is handled via callbacks, Promises, or async/await, allowing the main thread to remain responsive while waiting.

junior.question: What is a Promise in JavaScript and how does it work?
hints: A Promise represents the eventual result of an asynchronous operation
It has three states: pending, fulfilled, rejected
junior.answer: A Promise is an object representing the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation. It starts in the pending state and transitions to fulfilled (resolved with a value) or rejected (with a reason). .then() handles fulfillment, .catch() handles rejection, and .finally() runs regardless. Promises can be chained to sequence async operations. async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises, making asynchronous code read like synchronous code.

junior.question: How do you center an element both horizontally and vertically in CSS?
hints: Modern approach: use Flexbox or Grid with align-items and justify-content on the parent
Alternative: position absolute with transform: translate(-50%, -50%)
junior.answer: The cleanest modern approach is to set display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center on the parent container. CSS Grid achieves the same with display: grid; place-items: center. For absolute positioning, set the child to position: absolute; top: 50%; left: 50%; transform: translate(-50%, -50%). The Flexbox or Grid approach is preferred as it works without fixed dimensions and adapts to content size.

junior.question: What is the difference between display:none and visibility:hidden in CSS?
hints: display:none removes the element from layout entirely (no space taken)
visibility:hidden hides the element but preserves the space it occupies
junior.answer: display: none removes the element from the rendering tree completely — it takes up no space and is invisible. visibility: hidden hides the element visually but it still occupies its original space, leaving a gap where it would appear. A third option, opacity: 0, makes an element fully transparent but still interactive and space-occupying. Choose based on whether you want to preserve layout space or not.

junior.question: What is the difference between an HTML attribute and a DOM property?
hints: Attributes are defined in HTML markup and are always strings; properties are live JavaScript values on the DOM node
The value attribute sets the initial value of an input; the value property reflects the current value
junior.answer: Attributes are defined in the HTML source (e.g., <input type='text' value='hello'>) and are always strings; they represent the initial configuration. DOM properties are JavaScript object properties on the corresponding node — they can be any type and reflect the current state. For inputs, the value attribute sets the default value while the value property holds what the user has typed. Most attributes and properties share names but some differ (class attribute vs className property, for attribute vs htmlFor in React).

middle.question: How does the Virtual DOM work and what problem does it solve?
hints: Virtual DOM is an in-memory copy of the real DOM
Diffing algorithm compares old vs new virtual trees, then batches minimal real DOM updates
middle.answer: The Virtual DOM is a lightweight JS object tree mirroring the real DOM. On state change, a new virtual tree is produced and diffed against the previous one (reconciliation). Only the changed nodes are flushed to the real DOM as batched mutations. This avoids expensive full-page DOM rewrites, since JS object operations are orders of magnitude cheaper than direct DOM manipulation.

middle.question: What are Web Workers and when would you use them?
hints: Web Workers run scripts in a background thread, off the main thread
Good for CPU-intensive tasks like image processing, large data parsing
middle.answer: Web Workers execute JS in a background thread separate from the main UI thread, preventing CPU-heavy work from blocking rendering. They communicate via postMessage/onmessage and have no DOM access. Use them for image processing, large dataset sorting, cryptography, or parsing large JSON — anything that would cause visible jank on the main thread.

middle.question: What are common causes of memory leaks in JavaScript and how do you detect them?
hints: Forgotten timers/intervals, detached DOM nodes, closures holding large objects
Chrome DevTools Memory panel, heap snapshots
middle.answer: Common causes: forgotten setInterval callbacks holding closures, event listeners not removed on component unmount, closures retaining references to large objects, and detached DOM nodes still referenced in JS variables. Detect with Chrome DevTools Memory panel — compare heap snapshots before and after a suspected leak; growing retained objects indicate the leak source.

middle.question: What is CORS, why does the browser enforce it, and how do you configure it on the server?
hints: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing — browsers block cross-origin requests by default (same-origin policy)
Server sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin and other headers
middle.answer: CORS enforces the same-origin policy: a script on a.com cannot fetch from b.com unless b.com opts in. For non-simple requests the browser sends a preflight OPTIONS; the server must respond with Access-Control-Allow-Origin (and optionally Allow-Methods, Allow-Headers) matching the origin. This is browser-only — server-to-server requests are never blocked.

middle.question: What is the difference between HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2? How does HTTP/2 improve performance?
hints: HTTP/2 adds multiplexing (multiple requests over one TCP connection), header compression, server push
HTTP/1.1 suffers from head-of-line blocking
middle.answer: HTTP/1.1 uses separate TCP connections per request, causing head-of-line blocking. HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple streams over a single connection concurrently, compresses headers with HPACK, adds binary framing, and supports server push. The result is significantly lower latency for pages with many assets.

middle.question: Explain the JavaScript event loop. How do the call stack, task queue, and microtask queue interact?
hints: Microtasks (Promises) run before the next macrotask
Macrotasks: setTimeout, setInterval, I/O callbacks
middle.answer: The call stack executes synchronous code. When empty, the event loop drains the entire microtask queue (Promise .then, queueMicrotask) before picking the next macrotask (setTimeout, I/O) from the task queue. Promise callbacks always run before the next timer fires.

middle.question: What is the difference between debouncing and throttling? Provide use cases for each.
hints: Debounce: delay execution until after a quiet period — good for search input
Throttle: execute at most once per interval — good for scroll/resize handlers
middle.answer: Debounce delays a function until a quiet period has passed since the last call — ideal for search autocomplete (fire API only after typing stops). Throttle limits execution to once per interval regardless of call frequency — ideal for scroll/resize handlers where you want smooth, rate-limited updates.

middle.question: What are Service Workers? How do they enable Progressive Web Apps?
hints: Service Workers act as a network proxy running in the background
They enable offline support, background sync, and push notifications
middle.answer: A Service Worker is a background thread acting as a programmable network proxy: it intercepts fetch requests and can serve responses from the Cache API, enabling offline operation. Combined with Web Push and Background Sync, it powers the three PWA pillars: offline capability, push notifications, and background data sync.

middle.question: Explain lazy loading in the context of web applications. What are its benefits and trade-offs?
hints: Defer loading of images or code until needed (viewport entry or user action)
Benefits: faster initial load; trade-offs: complexity, potential layout shift
middle.answer: Lazy loading defers images, video, or JS chunks until they enter the viewport (IntersectionObserver or loading='lazy') or a route is navigated to. Benefits: faster initial load, lower bandwidth. Trade-offs: slight delay on first use, potential layout shift if dimensions are not reserved, and complexity for route code-splitting.

middle.question: How does React reconciliation work and what triggers a re-render?
hints: React diffs the new element tree against the previous fiber tree to find minimal changes
Re-renders are triggered by setState, context changes, or parent re-renders
middle.answer: React reconciliation compares the newly returned element tree with the previous fiber tree node by node. When types match, React updates the existing DOM node with new props; when types differ, it unmounts the old subtree and mounts the new one. Re-renders are triggered by: calling setState or a setter from useState, a consumed Context value changing, a parent re-rendering (causing all children to re-render by default), or external store subscriptions notifying. Keys help React identify which list items changed, moved, or were added.

middle.question: What is the difference between useState and useReducer in React?
hints: useState is best for simple, independent state values
useReducer is better for complex state with multiple sub-values or actions that depend on each other
middle.answer: useState manages a single value and is ideal for simple, independent state like a boolean toggle or a string input. useReducer takes a reducer function and initial state, returning current state and a dispatch function; it shines when next state depends on the previous state, when multiple related fields change together, or when logic is complex enough to extract to a pure function. useReducer also makes it easier to test state transitions in isolation.

middle.question: How do you clean up side effects in the useEffect hook?
hints: Return a cleanup function from useEffect; it runs before the next effect or on unmount
Common cleanups: clear timers, cancel subscriptions, abort fetch requests
middle.answer: useEffect can return a cleanup function that React calls before re-running the effect (if dependencies changed) and before the component unmounts. This prevents stale timers, event listeners, and subscriptions. For fetch requests, return a function that calls abortController.abort() to cancel in-flight requests on cleanup. Forgetting cleanup is the leading cause of memory leaks and stale-closure bugs in React components.

middle.question: When should you use React Context versus a state management library like Redux?
hints: Context is built in and suitable for low-frequency updates like theme or current user
Redux excels when many disconnected components need the same frequently-changing state
middle.answer: React Context is ideal for relatively static or low-frequency values — current user, theme, locale — shared across many component levels. It is simple and built in. Redux (or Redux Toolkit) suits applications with large, complex shared state that many disconnected components read and write frequently, where you need predictable state history, time-travel debugging, or middleware for side effects. Context triggers re-renders of all consumers on every change; Redux's connect/useSelector re-renders only when the selected slice changes.

middle.question: What are useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo and when should you use them?
hints: useMemo memoizes a computed value; useCallback memoizes a function reference
React.memo is a HOC that skips re-rendering if props have not changed (shallow comparison)
middle.answer: useMemo caches the result of an expensive computation between renders, recomputing only when specified dependencies change. useCallback caches a function reference, preventing child components that depend on referential equality from re-rendering unnecessarily. React.memo wraps a component and skips re-rendering if props shallowly compare as equal. Use them surgically to fix measured performance problems — premature memoization adds overhead and can introduce stale-closure bugs.

middle.question: Why are keys important in React lists and what happens when they are wrong or missing?
hints: Keys help React identify which list items changed, were added, or removed during reconciliation
Using index as key can cause incorrect rendering when items are reordered or filtered
middle.answer: Keys give React a stable identity for each list item during reconciliation. Without keys, or with duplicate/unstable keys, React falls back to positional diffing, causing incorrect DOM reuse — state from one component leaks into a different item's position. Using index as key breaks when the list is sorted or filtered because the same index now maps to a different item. Use a stable, unique identifier from the data (e.g., database ID) as the key.

middle.question: What is the React component lifecycle and how does it map to hooks?
hints: Class lifecycle: componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, componentWillUnmount
useEffect covers all three phases depending on its dependency array
middle.answer: Class components have lifecycle methods: componentDidMount (after first render), componentDidUpdate (after updates), componentWillUnmount (before removal). In function components, useEffect with an empty dependency array [] mimics componentDidMount; with dependencies, it runs after the first render and whenever dependencies change (componentDidUpdate); its cleanup return mimics componentWillUnmount. useState and useRef replace this.state and instance variables.

middle.question: How does code splitting work in React with React.lazy and Suspense?
hints: React.lazy(() => import('./Component')) dynamically imports a component
Suspense provides a fallback UI while the lazy chunk is loading
middle.answer: React.lazy accepts a function that returns a dynamic import() promise. React defers bundling that component into a separate chunk, loaded on demand when it is first rendered. Suspense wraps lazy components and renders a fallback (spinner, skeleton) while the chunk loads. Combined with route-based splitting, this reduces the initial bundle to only what is needed for the first page, accelerating time-to-interactive.

middle.question: What are the main features of Next.js and why would you choose it over plain React?
hints: Next.js provides file-system routing, SSR/SSG/ISR out of the box, and API routes
It also handles image optimisation, font loading, and automatic code splitting
middle.answer: Next.js is a React framework adding file-system based routing (every file in /pages or /app is a route), multiple rendering strategies (SSR, SSG, ISR) per page, built-in API routes, automatic code splitting, image optimisation via next/image, and font/script optimisation. It eliminates boilerplate configuration for these common needs. Choose Next.js when SEO, performance, or full-stack capabilities are required and you want a structured, opinionated project setup.

middle.question: What are TypeScript generics and when are they useful?
hints: Generics create reusable type-safe functions and data structures
Syntax: function identity<T>(arg: T): T { return arg; }
middle.answer: Generics let you write functions, interfaces, and classes that work with a type parameter rather than a hardcoded type, preserving type information through the operation. A generic identity function <T>(arg: T): T returns exactly the type passed in, unlike a function typed as (arg: any): any which loses type information. They are essential for reusable utilities (Array<T>, Promise<T>), API wrappers, and React component props that accept typed children.

middle.question: What is the difference between TypeScript union types and intersection types?
hints: Union (A | B) means a value can be one of several types
Intersection (A & B) means a value must satisfy all types simultaneously
middle.answer: A union type (A | B) means the value can be any one of the listed types; you typically narrow it with type guards before accessing type-specific properties. An intersection type (A & B) means the value must satisfy all listed types simultaneously, effectively merging them — useful for combining interface shapes (e.g., User & Admin gives an object with all properties of both). Unions model 'or'; intersections model 'and'.

middle.question: What does Webpack do and what are its core concepts?
hints: Webpack is a module bundler that transforms, bundles, and optimises assets
Core concepts: entry, output, loaders (transform files), plugins (extend build process)
middle.answer: Webpack analyses a dependency graph starting from entry points, transforms files through loaders (Babel for JS, css-loader for CSS, file-loader for images), and bundles everything into output files optimised for the browser. Plugins extend the build: HtmlWebpackPlugin generates the HTML, MiniCssExtractPlugin extracts CSS, and TerserPlugin minifies JS. Mode (development/production) toggles sensible defaults. Modern alternatives (Vite, Parcel, esbuild) offer faster builds by leveraging native ES modules.

middle.question: What are CSS Modules and what problem do they solve?
hints: CSS Modules locally scope class names by auto-generating unique identifiers
Prevents style collisions between components in large applications
middle.answer: CSS Modules transform class names at build time by appending a unique hash (e.g., .button becomes .button_abc123), making styles locally scoped to the component that imports them. This eliminates the global CSS collision problem in large applications where unrelated components might share class names. They are plain CSS files but imported as objects in JS — styles.button — so they compose naturally and are fully typed in TypeScript with generated type definitions.

middle.question: What is Tailwind CSS and how does its utility-first approach differ from traditional CSS?
hints: Tailwind provides low-level utility classes applied directly in HTML (e.g., bg-blue-500 p-4)
Removes the need to name CSS classes or maintain a separate stylesheet for most cases
middle.answer: Tailwind CSS is a utility-first framework providing single-purpose classes (flex, pt-4, text-center) applied directly in the markup rather than writing custom CSS. Unlike component-based frameworks (Bootstrap), Tailwind does not provide pre-styled components — you compose them from utilities. At build time, PurgeCSS removes unused utilities, resulting in a tiny production stylesheet. It trades CSS file maintenance for HTML verbosity, which many teams find more maintainable at scale.

middle.question: How does React prevent XSS, and where are you still vulnerable?
hints: React escapes values rendered via JSX by default
Vulnerabilities: dangerouslySetInnerHTML, href='javascript:...', third-party libraries
middle.answer: React escapes all string values rendered through JSX expressions, converting angle brackets and quotes to HTML entities before inserting them in the DOM. This prevents injected HTML from running. Remaining vectors: dangerouslySetInnerHTML bypasses escaping entirely and requires sanitisation (DOMPurify) before use; href with user input can accept javascript: URLs — always validate URL protocols; third-party dependencies may have their own XSS bugs.

middle.question: What is CSRF and how is it prevented in frontend applications?
hints: CSRF tricks authenticated users into making unintended requests to a site where they are logged in
Prevention: SameSite cookies, CSRF tokens, checking the Origin/Referer header
middle.answer: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) exploits the fact that browsers automatically include cookies with requests. An attacker's page submits a form to the victim's bank, and the browser attaches the victim's session cookie. Prevention: set session cookies with SameSite=Strict or Lax (blocks cross-origin form submissions); use a CSRF token (a secret value the attacker cannot read) embedded in forms and validated server-side; check the Origin/Referer header for non-idempotent requests.

middle.question: What is the Intersection Observer API and what are its use cases?
hints: IntersectionObserver asynchronously reports when an element enters or exits the viewport
Use cases: lazy loading images, infinite scroll, scroll-triggered animations, analytics visibility tracking
middle.answer: The Intersection Observer API calls a callback asynchronously whenever a target element's visibility relative to a root (usually the viewport) crosses specified thresholds. It runs off the main thread, making it far more efficient than polling getBoundingClientRect in a scroll listener. Primary uses: lazy loading images, triggering infinite scroll pagination, animating elements on entry, and tracking ad/content viewability for analytics.

middle.question: What is the difference between CSS animations and the Web Animations API?
hints: CSS animations are declarative (@keyframes + animation properties)
Web Animations API is imperative JavaScript, giving dynamic control over playback
middle.answer: CSS animations are defined declaratively with @keyframes and applied via animation-* properties — straightforward for static, predefined sequences. The Web Animations API (WAAPI) is a JavaScript interface: element.animate(keyframes, options) returns an Animation object with play(), pause(), and cancel() controls and Promise-based events. WAAPI enables dynamic, data-driven animations, precise synchronisation between multiple elements, and better integration with JavaScript application logic.

middle.question: What is the difference between browser reflow (layout) and repaint? Why does it matter for performance?
hints: Reflow recalculates geometry of elements and is expensive; repaint redraws pixels without geometry changes
Causes of reflow: changing width/height, adding/removing DOM nodes, reading layout properties like offsetWidth
middle.answer: Reflow (layout) recalculates the size and position of every affected element in the document — a potentially large subtree operation. Repaint redraws pixels (colour, visibility) without recalculating geometry — cheaper than reflow but still not free. Reading layout properties (offsetWidth, scrollTop) after DOM mutations forces synchronous layout, causing layout thrashing. Batch DOM writes, use transform and opacity for animations (GPU composited, no reflow or repaint), and use will-change to promote elements to separate compositor layers.

middle.question: What is render-blocking in the browser and how do you eliminate it?
hints: Scripts and stylesheets in <head> block HTML parsing and first render
Use async/defer for scripts; inline critical CSS; preload key resources
middle.answer: Render-blocking occurs when the browser pauses HTML parsing to download and execute a resource before it can paint. External CSS is render-blocking by default; a script without async or defer blocks parsing at the point of the tag. Solutions: inline critical CSS to avoid a round-trip; add defer to non-critical scripts (executes after parsing, in order) or async (executes immediately when downloaded, no order guarantee); use link rel='preload' for critical fonts and images to fetch them earlier.

middle.question: What is the difference between progressive enhancement and graceful degradation?
hints: Progressive enhancement builds a baseline that works everywhere, then layers enhancements for capable browsers
Graceful degradation starts with full features and degrades for older browsers
middle.answer: Progressive enhancement starts with a functional baseline (semantic HTML, core CSS) that works in any browser, then enhances with JavaScript, animations, and advanced CSS for capable browsers. The feature is usable for everyone, just richer in modern environments. Graceful degradation designs for modern browsers first and adds fallbacks for older ones. Progressive enhancement is considered more robust because the core path is always tested and reliable; graceful degradation risks the fallback never being properly exercised.

middle.question: What is a design system and what are its key components?
hints: A design system is a centralised collection of reusable components and design guidelines
Key parts: component library, design tokens, documentation (e.g., Storybook), contribution guidelines
middle.answer: A design system is a single source of truth for a product's UI: it includes design tokens (colour, spacing, typography scales), a reusable component library, usage guidelines, accessibility standards, and documentation. It ensures visual consistency across products and teams, reduces duplicated work, and accelerates development. Tools: Figma for design tokens, Storybook for component documentation and isolated development, and a published npm package for code distribution.

middle.question: What is Storybook and how does it help frontend development?
hints: Storybook provides an isolated sandbox to develop and document UI components independently of the application
Stories define component states; Storybook can run visual regression tests with add-ons like Chromatic
middle.answer: Storybook is an open-source tool that renders UI components in isolation, outside the main application context. Each 'story' represents a component in a specific state (primary button, disabled button, loading button). This enables development without needing to wire up the full app, facilitates visual review by designers, serves as living documentation, and integrates with visual regression testing tools like Chromatic that detect unintended appearance changes across branches.

middle.question: What does React Testing Library encourage and how does it differ from Enzyme?
hints: RTL tests what the user sees and interacts with, not implementation details
Enzyme allows shallow rendering and direct access to component internals
middle.answer: React Testing Library (RTL) promotes testing from the user's perspective: query by accessible role, label, or text rather than component internals or CSS classes. This makes tests resilient to implementation changes. Enzyme allowed shallow rendering individual components in isolation and direct access to state and lifecycle methods — tests were tightly coupled to implementation. RTL's philosophy: if a refactor does not change user-visible behaviour, tests should not break.

middle.question: What is snapshot testing and what are its advantages and limitations?
hints: Snapshot tests serialise a component's rendered output and compare against a stored snapshot on future runs
Advantage: catches unintended regressions; limitation: large snapshots are hard to review and can be blindly updated
middle.answer: Snapshot testing renders a component, serialises the output to a string, and saves it to a .snap file. Future test runs compare current output against the stored snapshot and fail on differences. Benefits: easy to set up, catches accidental regressions in markup. Limitations: snapshots become large and unreadable, are often updated blindly without human review, and test what the code does rather than what it should do — failing to cover intent. Best used for small, stable leaf components.

middle.question: How do Cypress and Jest differ in scope and purpose?
hints: Jest is a unit/integration test runner executed in Node.js (jsdom or real Node)
Cypress is an end-to-end test framework that drives a real browser
middle.answer: Jest is a JavaScript test runner for unit and integration tests, running in Node.js (with jsdom for DOM simulations). It is fast, runs in parallel, and is ideal for testing functions, hooks, services, and component logic in isolation. Cypress runs tests inside a real browser with a full DOM, network, and rendering pipeline — ideal for end-to-end tests that simulate real user journeys. Jest tests are faster and deterministic; Cypress tests are slower but test the full integrated stack.

middle.question: Compare Zustand and Redux for state management in a React application.
hints: Zustand is minimal and unopinionated — a single hook, no boilerplate
Redux (Toolkit) provides structured patterns, middleware, and DevTools for complex state
middle.answer: Zustand is a minimal library: define a store with a function, read state with a hook, update it by calling setters. No actions, reducers, or providers required. It is ideal for small-to-medium apps or module-level state. Redux Toolkit provides a structured approach: slices, reducers, action creators, thunk middleware, and the Redux DevTools for time-travel debugging. Its predictable patterns pay off in large teams and complex state interactions. Zustand prioritises simplicity; Redux prioritises structure and observability.

middle.question: What are React Portals and when would you use them?
hints: ReactDOM.createPortal renders a child component into a different DOM node than the parent
Common use cases: modals, tooltips, dropdowns that need to escape CSS overflow:hidden or z-index constraints
middle.answer: React Portals render a component's output into a specified DOM node that exists outside the component's parent DOM hierarchy. Event bubbling still works through the React component tree, not the DOM tree. Use cases: modals and dialogs that must overlay the entire page (escaping overflow:hidden containers), tooltips and dropdowns that need to render at the body level to avoid z-index and clipping issues, and third-party widget containers.

middle.question: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled components in React?
hints: Controlled: form input value is driven by React state via value and onChange
Uncontrolled: the DOM maintains its own state, accessed via a ref
middle.answer: A controlled component binds its value to React state: the input's value prop is set from state and onChange updates state, making React the single source of truth. An uncontrolled component stores its value in the DOM; you access it via a ref when needed (e.g., on form submit). Controlled components enable instant validation, conditional disabling, and synchronisation between inputs. Uncontrolled components are simpler for simple cases or when integrating with non-React code.

middle.question: What is prop drilling and what are the alternatives?
hints: Prop drilling passes data through many intermediate components that do not need it
Alternatives: React Context, state management libraries, component composition
middle.answer: Prop drilling occurs when a value must be passed through several intermediate components purely to reach a deeply nested consumer. It makes intermediate components artificially coupled to data they do not use. Alternatives: React Context for low-frequency shared values, Zustand or Redux for global state, or component composition (passing JSX as children/props to avoid intermediaries). Composition is often the most underused and elegant solution.

middle.question: What are React custom hooks and what are the rules for writing them?
hints: Custom hooks are functions starting with 'use' that can call other hooks
They encapsulate and reuse stateful logic without changing the component hierarchy
middle.answer: Custom hooks are JavaScript functions prefixed with use that can call built-in hooks (useState, useEffect, etc.). They extract and reuse stateful logic across components without render props or HOCs. Rules: only call hooks at the top level (not inside loops, conditions, or nested functions) and only call hooks from React function components or other custom hooks. Custom hooks return whatever is useful — values, setters, event handlers.

middle.question: What are the Core Web Vitals and what does each measure?
hints: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint), INP (Interaction to Next Paint), CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift)
They are Google's key user-experience metrics used in search ranking
middle.answer: Core Web Vitals are Google's primary UX performance metrics. LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) measures loading performance — the time for the largest visible image or text block to render; target under 2.5 s. INP (Interaction to Next Paint) measures responsiveness — the latency from user interaction to next visual update; target under 200 ms. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) measures visual stability — the sum of unexpected layout shifts; target under 0.1. They influence both user experience and Google search ranking.

middle.question: What is HTTP caching and how do Cache-Control directives work?
hints: Cache-Control: max-age=3600 tells browsers to cache the response for an hour
no-cache requires revalidation; no-store prevents caching entirely; immutable marks assets that never change
middle.answer: HTTP caching stores server responses to serve future requests faster. Cache-Control: max-age=N marks a response fresh for N seconds — the browser serves it without a network request. After expiry, the browser revalidates using ETag or Last-Modified headers. no-cache means always revalidate before using cached response. no-store disables caching entirely. immutable (used with content-hashed filenames) tells the browser the file never changes, allowing aggressive long-term caching without stale-content risk.

middle.question: What is the composition pattern in React and why is it preferred over inheritance?
hints: Composition uses children and render props to share behaviour between components
React's own docs recommend composition over class inheritance for sharing UI and logic
middle.answer: Composition builds complex components by combining simpler ones rather than inheriting from base classes. React components accept children and can render arbitrary UI passed as props, enabling flexible, loosely coupled designs. Common patterns: children-as-slot, render props, and HOCs. Inheritance struggles with deep chains and tight coupling; composition is more transparent and testable. React's own composition model via the component tree replaces the need for inheritance hierarchies found in class-based OOP.

middle.question: What are TypeScript utility types and which ones do you use most often?
hints: Common utility types: Partial<T>, Required<T>, Readonly<T>, Pick<T,K>, Omit<T,K>, Record<K,V>
They transform existing types without redefining them
middle.answer: TypeScript utility types transform existing types. Partial<T> makes all properties optional; Required<T> makes all required; Readonly<T> prevents mutation. Pick<T, K> creates a type with only the listed keys; Omit<T, K> creates a type excluding them. Record<K, V> creates a dictionary type. ReturnType<T> extracts a function's return type; Parameters<T> extracts its parameter tuple. These reduce boilerplate and keep types DRY when deriving related types from a single source of truth.

middle.question: What are React Error Boundaries and how do you implement one?
hints: Error Boundaries catch JavaScript errors in the component tree and display a fallback UI
They must be class components using componentDidCatch and getDerivedStateFromError
middle.answer: Error Boundaries are class components that catch runtime errors thrown during rendering, lifecycle methods, or constructors of their child tree, displaying a fallback UI instead of a blank/crashed screen. They implement static getDerivedStateFromError() to update state on error (triggering fallback render) and componentDidCatch() to log the error. Function components cannot be Error Boundaries yet — the react-error-boundary library provides a hook-friendly wrapper. Wrap each page or major section separately to isolate crashes.

middle.question: What are common patterns for form validation in React applications?
hints: Uncontrolled validation with HTML5 constraints (required, pattern), controlled validation with state, or libraries (React Hook Form, Zod)
Validation should occur on blur and on submit; inline error messages must be accessible
middle.answer: Form validation can be handled at multiple levels: HTML5 constraint attributes (required, minlength, pattern) for basic validation without JS; controlled state with validation logic in onChange or onBlur handlers; or form libraries like React Hook Form, which use uncontrolled inputs internally for performance and integrate with schema validators like Zod or Yup. Whichever approach is used, associate error messages with inputs via aria-describedby and only show errors after the user has interacted with the field.

middle.question: What is optimistic UI and when is it appropriate to use it?
hints: Optimistic UI immediately applies the expected result of an action before the server confirms it
Roll back to previous state if the server returns an error
middle.answer: Optimistic UI applies the expected outcome of a user action instantly in the UI, assuming it will succeed, then confirms or rolls back when the server responds. It makes interactions feel instantaneous — toggling a like, reordering a list, deleting an item. It is appropriate when the success rate is very high and rollback on failure is straightforward. It requires careful error handling: display a meaningful error and revert the UI if the request fails, avoiding silent data inconsistencies.

middle.question: How do you ensure accessible React applications?
hints: Use semantic HTML elements, proper ARIA attributes, and ensure keyboard navigability
Test with screen readers (NVDA, VoiceOver) and automated tools like axe-core
middle.answer: Accessibility in React starts with semantic HTML — use buttons for actions, links for navigation, and form elements with associated labels. Manage focus programmatically when content changes (modals, route changes). Use ARIA attributes (role, aria-label, aria-live) only where native semantics are insufficient. Ensure all interactive elements are keyboard-operable. Integrate axe-core or eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y in the development pipeline to catch issues early. Test with real screen readers and keyboard-only navigation.

middle.question: What are bundle analysis tools and how do you use them to optimise bundle size?
hints: webpack-bundle-analyzer and source-map-explorer visualise what code ends up in your bundles
Look for duplicate dependencies, unexpectedly large packages, and unnecessary polyfills
middle.answer: Bundle analysis tools like webpack-bundle-analyzer generate interactive treemap visualisations of your output bundles, showing exactly which modules contribute to their size. source-map-explorer does the same from source maps. Use them to identify: large dependencies where a smaller alternative exists (moment.js vs date-fns), duplicate packages from version mismatches, entire modules imported when only one function is needed (lodash vs lodash-es), and unnecessary polyfills. Run analysis in CI to enforce size budgets and catch regressions early.

middle.question: What is the difference between a named export and a default export in ES modules?
hints: A module can have multiple named exports but only one default export
Named imports use curly braces: import { foo } from './foo'; default imports do not: import foo from './foo'
middle.answer: Named exports export a binding by a specific name — a module can have as many as needed: export const foo = 1; export function bar() {}. They are imported with destructuring syntax and the name must match. A default export exports a single value without a name: export default function(). It is imported with any name the consumer chooses. Named exports are preferred for tree shaking because bundlers can precisely identify and remove individual unused exports, whereas a default-exported object is treated as a single opaque value.

senior.question: Describe the browser rendering pipeline from HTML bytes to pixels on screen.
hints: Parse HTML → DOM, parse CSS → CSSOM, merge → Render Tree, Layout, Paint, Composite
JavaScript can block parsing; async/defer attributes mitigate this
senior.answer: Bytes → tokenise → DOM. In parallel, CSS → CSSOM. DOM + CSSOM → Render Tree (visible nodes only). Layout calculates geometry. Paint records drawing commands. Compositing assembles layers on the GPU. JS is render-blocking by default; async/defer shift it post-parse. Forced synchronous layouts (read then write in a loop) interleave layout and script execution, causing jank.

senior.question: What optimizations does the V8 engine apply to JavaScript? How does knowing them influence how you write code?
hints: JIT compilation, hidden classes, inline caching, garbage collection (generational)
Avoid polymorphic functions, deoptimization triggers, excessive object shape changes
senior.answer: V8 JIT-compiles hot paths to native code. Objects with the same property-initialisation order share a hidden class enabling fast access; adding properties post-construction changes the shape and deoptimises. Inline caching speeds repeated calls with identical argument types; polymorphic call sites are slower. Generational GC cheaply collects short-lived objects. Practical rule: initialise all properties in the constructor and write monomorphic functions.

senior.question: What are micro-frontends? What problems do they solve and what challenges do they introduce?
hints: Decompose a frontend app into independently deployable pieces owned by separate teams
Challenges: shared state, CSS isolation, performance (multiple frameworks), routing
senior.answer: Micro-frontends split a large UI into independently deployable vertical slices, each owned by a separate team with its own tech stack and release cycle. Implementation options: Module Federation, iframes, web components, server-side composition. Challenges: CSS namespace collisions, duplicated framework bundles inflating total JS, shared state coordination, consistent UX, and cross-slice routing.

senior.question: What is WebAssembly (WASM)? In what scenarios would you use it instead of JavaScript?
hints: WASM is a binary instruction format running at near-native speed in the browser
Suitable for compute-heavy tasks: video codecs, 3D engines, cryptography
senior.answer: WebAssembly is a binary format executing at near-native speed in the browser sandbox. It complements JS: JS orchestrates the app, WASM handles compute-heavy work — video/audio codecs, 3D rendering (Figma, Unity Web), cryptography, or porting C/C++/Rust libraries without rewriting them. It communicates with JS via shared linear memory and exported functions.

senior.question: What is the Critical Rendering Path and how would you optimize it for a production application?
hints: Minimize render-blocking resources, inline critical CSS, defer non-critical JS
Preload key resources, use font-display:swap, reduce main-thread work
senior.answer: The CRP is the sequence from HTML bytes to first pixel: parse HTML, parse CSS, layout, paint. Optimisations: inline above-the-fold CSS; defer/async non-critical scripts; preload key fonts and hero images; use font-display:swap; reduce DOM depth. Measure with Lighthouse and Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS).

senior.question: How does tree shaking work in modern bundlers like Webpack or Rollup? What are its limitations?
hints: Static analysis of ES module import/export graph to eliminate dead code
Limitations: side effects, CommonJS modules, dynamic imports
senior.answer: Tree shaking statically analyses the ES module import/export graph to remove unused exports. Limitations: CommonJS (require()) is not statically analysable. Modules with side effects must be marked in package.json (sideEffects: false or a list) to prevent incorrect removal. Dynamic require(variable) calls are opaque to the analyser.

senior.question: Explain Content Security Policy (CSP). How does it prevent XSS and what are its trade-offs?
hints: CSP is an HTTP header that whitelists allowed sources for scripts, styles, images, etc.
Trade-offs: complexity, breaking inline scripts, maintenance overhead
senior.answer: CSP is an HTTP header declaring approved sources for scripts, styles, and other resources. It prevents XSS by blocking inline script execution and untrusted origins — even injected HTML cannot run code. Trade-offs: inline scripts must move to external files or use nonces/hashes; third-party integrations require ongoing whitelist maintenance; unsafe-inline defeats the protection entirely.

senior.question: Compare Server-Side Rendering (SSR), Static Site Generation (SSG), and Client-Side Rendering (CSR). When would you choose each?
hints: SSR: dynamic data per request, good for SEO, adds server cost
SSG: pre-built HTML at build time, fastest delivery; CSR: rich interactivity, poor initial SEO
senior.answer: CSR delivers a bare HTML shell and renders in the browser — great interactivity, poor SEO, slow first paint. SSR renders full HTML per request server-side — good SEO, fast first paint, adds server cost. SSG pre-builds HTML at deploy time and serves from CDN — fastest delivery, best SEO, requires rebuilds for content changes. Choose SSG for static content, SSR for user-specific data, CSR for heavily interactive apps behind auth.

senior.question: How would you architect a large-scale React application for performance, maintainability, and team scalability?
hints: Feature-based folder structure, code splitting, state management strategy (Zustand, Redux Toolkit)
Monorepo (Nx/Turborepo), design system, contract testing between micro-frontends
senior.answer: Feature-based folder structure co-locating components, hooks, tests, and types. Route-level code splitting with React.lazy. State management by complexity: Zustand/Jotai for lightweight, Redux Toolkit for complex shared state. Multi-team scale: monorepo (Turborepo/Nx), shared design system package, enforced API contracts, performance budgets in CI (Lighthouse, bundle-size checks).

senior.question: How do you architect a design system intended for consumption by multiple product teams at scale?
hints: Design tokens as the foundation; component library versioned and published as an npm package
Governance model: contribution guidelines, changelog, versioning, visual regression testing
senior.answer: Start with design tokens (colour, spacing, typography, shadows) shared between design (Figma variables) and code. Build a component library published as a versioned npm package with TypeScript declarations and full Storybook documentation. Establish a governance model: a core team owns the library, contribution guidelines allow product teams to propose additions, semantic versioning and a changelog communicate breaking changes. Automated visual regression tests (Chromatic) catch unintended appearance changes on every PR.

senior.question: What is Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR) in Next.js and how does it work?
hints: ISR regenerates individual static pages in the background at a specified revalidation interval
Users always receive a cached page instantly; stale-while-revalidate pattern
senior.answer: ISR extends SSG by allowing pages to be regenerated after deployment without rebuilding the entire site. Setting revalidate: N in getStaticProps means: after N seconds, the next request to that page triggers a background regeneration. The current cached version is served immediately (stale-while-revalidate), and once regeneration completes the new version replaces it. This balances the delivery speed of static pages with reasonably fresh dynamic content, eliminating full rebuilds for content updates.

senior.question: What are React Server Components and how do they differ from Client Components?
hints: Server Components render on the server with zero client-side JS; they can fetch data directly
Client Components are hydrated in the browser and can use hooks and event listeners
senior.answer: React Server Components (RSC) render exclusively on the server and contribute zero JavaScript to the client bundle — they can directly access databases, file systems, and secrets. They cannot use hooks or browser APIs. Client Components (marked with 'use client') are sent to the browser, hydrated, and can use state, effects, and event handlers. The default in Next.js App Router is Server Component; you opt into client rendering only where interactivity is required, minimising JS shipped to the browser.

senior.question: What is the Islands Architecture and what problem does it solve?
hints: Islands are isolated interactive components embedded in an otherwise static server-rendered page
Used by Astro, Fresh — reduces JavaScript to only the components that need it
senior.answer: Islands Architecture renders the page as mostly static HTML on the server, with discrete interactive regions ('islands') hydrated independently on the client. Only island components ship JavaScript; the surrounding static content has zero JS overhead. This solves the over-hydration problem of traditional SSR frameworks that hydrate the entire page tree. Frameworks like Astro and Deno Fresh implement this model, making it ideal for content-heavy sites with occasional interactive widgets.

senior.question: How would you perform a deep-dive optimisation of Core Web Vitals for a production Next.js application?
hints: LCP: optimise hero image (priority, srcset, WebP), reduce TTFB with SSG/ISR, inline critical CSS
INP: reduce long tasks (code split, defer non-critical), CLS: reserve space for dynamic content (images, ads, fonts)
senior.answer: LCP: use next/image with priority on the hero image, serve WebP/AVIF, optimise TTFB via SSG/ISR, and preconnect to critical origins. INP: profile with Chrome DevTools Performance panel, break long tasks with scheduler.yield or setTimeout(0), move heavy work off the main thread with Web Workers. CLS: always set explicit width/height on images, use font-display:optional or swap with size-adjust, reserve space for lazy-loaded ads and skeleton screens. Track all three with Real User Monitoring (RUM) in production, not just Lighthouse.

senior.question: How does Module Federation in Webpack 5 work and what does it enable?
