hints: Module Federation allows separately deployed builds to share code and dependencies at runtime
Enables true micro-frontend architectures where each app is independently built and deployed
senior.answer: Module Federation allows a Webpack build to expose modules that other builds (running in the browser) can load dynamically at runtime from a separate URL. A host application consumes remote modules from other deployed applications without bundling them at build time. Shared dependencies (React, etc.) can be deduplicated across host and remotes. This enables micro-frontends where teams deploy independently and the shell application composes them at runtime, with no shared build step required.

senior.question: How would you implement real-time data updates in a React application? Compare WebSockets, Server-Sent Events, and polling.
hints: WebSockets: full-duplex, best for chat/collaborative apps
SSE: server-to-client only, simpler, works over HTTP/2; polling: simplest but wasteful
senior.answer: Polling periodically fetches from a REST endpoint — simple but wasteful and introduces latency up to the poll interval. Server-Sent Events (SSE) maintain a one-directional HTTP stream from server to client — ideal for live feeds, notifications, and dashboards; they work over HTTP/2 multiplexing and auto-reconnect. WebSockets provide full-duplex communication over a persistent TCP connection — necessary for collaborative editing, live chat, or multiplayer where the client also streams data. Use SSE unless bidirectional communication is required; it is simpler and firewall-friendlier.

senior.question: How do you implement and manage Dark Mode at scale in a large application?
hints: Use CSS custom properties (variables) as design tokens, toggled via a data attribute or class on <html>
Respect the OS preference with prefers-color-scheme; persist user choice in localStorage
senior.answer: Define all colour tokens as CSS custom properties on :root. Create a [data-theme='dark'] selector that overrides those variables with dark values. On load, read localStorage for a saved preference and fall back to the OS prefers-color-scheme media query. A small synchronous script in <head> (before CSS) sets the attribute to prevent flash of incorrect theme. In React, a context provides the toggle and current mode to any component that needs it. Ensure WCAG contrast ratios are validated for both themes in the design system.

senior.question: How do you architect a headless CMS solution for a large-scale Next.js application?
hints: Headless CMS (Contentful, Sanity, Strapi) decouples content management from presentation
Combine with ISR: pages regenerate when content changes via webhooks, not on a timer
senior.answer: A headless CMS exposes content via API, decoupled from the frontend. In Next.js: fetch content at build time with getStaticProps (SSG) for purely static pages, or use ISR with on-demand revalidation — the CMS triggers a Next.js revalidation webhook on publish, regenerating only the affected pages immediately. This combines instant delivery (CDN-cached static HTML) with live-ish content updates. Content preview mode serves draft content via getServerSideProps with a secret token, bypassing the static cache.

senior.question: What is Edge computing for frontend and what use cases does it unlock?
hints: Edge functions run at CDN nodes geographically close to users, reducing latency for personalisation and A/B testing
Platforms: Vercel Edge Functions, Cloudflare Workers, Netlify Edge Functions
senior.answer: Edge computing runs server-side code at CDN nodes worldwide rather than a single origin, reducing latency by processing requests close to the user. For frontends, this enables: personalised HTML without full SSR latency (geolocation-based content, A/B test variants), authentication and redirect logic without a round-trip to the origin, and edge caching with request transformation. Limitations: restricted Node.js APIs (no native modules), cold start constraints, and debugging complexity compared to conventional servers.

senior.question: How would you design and implement an A/B testing framework for a large React application?
hints: Assign users to variants server-side or at the edge to avoid layout shift; persist assignment in a cookie
Track exposure and conversion events to a data pipeline; feature flags power the variants
senior.answer: Assign users to experiment variants deterministically (hash of user ID) at the edge or in the SSR layer, persisting the assignment in a cookie to prevent flickering. In React, a context/provider reads the variant and exposes it to components. Decouple experiment logic using a feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash). Instrument exposure events (which variant was shown) and conversion events (goal completed) to a data pipeline. Ensure mutual exclusivity if experiments overlap, and establish a minimum detectable effect and sample size before launching.

senior.question: How do you handle internationalisation (i18n) at scale in a React application?
hints: Use a library like react-i18next or next-intl; extract all strings to locale files
Consider locale detection, pluralisation rules, RTL layout support, and number/date formatting
senior.answer: Extract all user-visible strings to per-locale JSON files and load them via a library (react-i18next, next-intl, FormatJS). Detect the locale from the Accept-Language header at the edge or SSR layer, persist in a cookie, and serve locale-specific content. Handle pluralisation via ICU message syntax. Number, date, and currency formatting use the Intl API (locale-aware). RTL languages require layout mirroring — use CSS logical properties (margin-inline-start instead of margin-left) and test with an Arabic or Hebrew locale. Automate missing translation detection in CI.

senior.question: How do Web Components compare to React components, and when would you choose them?
hints: Web Components are native browser APIs: Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, HTML Templates
React components require a runtime; Web Components work in any framework or vanilla HTML
senior.answer: Web Components are a set of native browser standards — Custom Elements register new HTML tags, Shadow DOM encapsulates styles and markup, and HTML Templates define inert fragments. They run without a framework and interoperate across React, Vue, Angular, or vanilla HTML. Choose Web Components for truly framework-agnostic UI primitives (like a shared component across multiple tech stacks in a micro-frontend) or for building a design system consumed by teams using different frameworks. For application logic, React's composability, hooks, and ecosystem outweigh Web Components' interoperability advantages.

senior.question: What is the Shadow DOM and what does it provide in terms of encapsulation?
hints: Shadow DOM attaches a separate, encapsulated DOM tree to an element
Styles inside a shadow root do not leak out, and external styles do not bleed in by default
senior.answer: The Shadow DOM attaches a separate sub-DOM tree (shadow tree) to an element (the shadow host). CSS within the shadow root is scoped — it cannot affect or be affected by the outer document's styles (though CSS custom properties pierce the boundary). This enables true style encapsulation for Web Components. The shadow tree is not part of the main document's querySelector scope unless explicitly queried. Event retargeting means events from inside the shadow tree appear to originate from the host element to outside listeners.

senior.question: How do you implement accessible complex components like comboboxes, date pickers, and data grids?
hints: Follow the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG) patterns for each widget type
Manage focus explicitly, implement keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Home, End, Escape), and use live regions for dynamic updates
senior.answer: Complex interactive components must follow ARIA Authoring Practices Guide patterns. A combobox combines role=combobox on the input with a role=listbox popup and aria-expanded/aria-activedescendant for screen reader tracking. Date pickers use a grid of role=gridcell with keyboard navigation (arrow keys traverse dates, Escape closes). Data grids implement the grid role with row/gridcell children. Focus management is critical: trap focus inside modal-like widgets, restore focus on close. Test every interaction keyboard-only and with NVDA + Firefox and VoiceOver + Safari.

senior.question: How do you optimise web fonts for performance and reduce layout shift?
hints: Use font-display: swap or optional; subset fonts to only used characters; self-host or preconnect to font origins
Use the size-adjust descriptor to match fallback font metrics and eliminate CLS
senior.answer: Font optimisations: subset fonts to only the characters used in your language to reduce file size (pyftsubset or Google Fonts subsetting parameters). Self-host fonts to avoid third-party DNS lookups; if using a CDN, add link rel='preconnect'. Use font-display: swap for important text (text visible immediately, font swaps in) or optional for non-critical (skips load if the connection is slow). To eliminate CLS from font swap, use the size-adjust and ascent-override descriptors to match the fallback font's metrics exactly to the web font.

senior.question: Describe the optimistic update pattern in depth, including how to handle conflicts and rollbacks.
hints: Apply the change locally and immediately, then confirm or revert on server response
Use a unique transaction ID to correlate in-flight requests with their local mutations
senior.answer: Assign each mutation a unique ID, immediately apply it to local state (optimistically), and track it as 'pending'. On successful server response, mark it confirmed and optionally replace local data with the canonical server response. On error, revert the mutation by restoring the previous state snapshot and display an actionable error (Retry / Dismiss). For concurrent mutations, queue them and apply sequentially or implement a conflict resolution strategy (last-write-wins vs operational transform). Libraries like React Query and SWR provide built-in optimistic update primitives with automatic rollback.

senior.question: How do you harden a frontend application against common security vulnerabilities beyond XSS?
hints: Implement CSP, Subresource Integrity (SRI), secure cookie flags, and avoid sensitive data in the client bundle
Address clickjacking (X-Frame-Options), open redirects, and dependency vulnerabilities (npm audit)
senior.answer: Security hardening beyond XSS: deploy a strict CSP with nonces/hashes to block injected scripts; use Subresource Integrity (integrity attribute) on third-party scripts and stylesheets to verify their content. Set cookies with Secure, HttpOnly, and SameSite=Strict. Add X-Frame-Options: DENY or CSP frame-ancestors to prevent clickjacking. Validate all redirect targets against an allowlist to prevent open redirect attacks. Run npm audit in CI and a tool like Snyk for ongoing dependency vulnerability scanning. Never put API keys, secrets, or PII in the client-side bundle.

senior.question: What is the Signals pattern (as used in Preact Signals, SolidJS) and how does it differ from React's state model?
hints: Signals are reactive primitives that automatically track dependents and update only what reads them
Unlike React, there is no virtual DOM diffing — updates are surgically precise
senior.answer: Signals are fine-grained reactive values that track which computations read them. When a signal's value changes, only the exact DOM nodes or effects that read it are updated — no virtual DOM diffing, no component re-render tree. In Preact Signals, passing a signal directly as a JSX prop causes only that text node to update. In SolidJS, the entire rendering model is based on signals. React's model re-renders components (subtrees) on state change and reconciles via the virtual DOM; signals bypass this entirely for surgical updates, yielding excellent performance without memoization.

senior.question: How do you implement infinite scrolling efficiently in React without degrading performance?
hints: Use IntersectionObserver to detect when the sentinel element enters the viewport
Virtualise the list with react-virtual or @tanstack/virtual to render only visible rows
senior.answer: Attach an IntersectionObserver to a sentinel element at the bottom of the list; when it enters the viewport, fetch the next page. Use a library like React Query or SWR for paginated fetching with built-in cache and deduplication. As the list grows, rendering thousands of DOM nodes degrades performance — use list virtualisation (react-virtual, @tanstack/virtual) to render only the visible rows plus a small overscan. Ensure the page size and row height are tuned so the initial render fills the viewport without empty space.

senior.question: What is the difference between a Shared Worker, a Service Worker, and a Dedicated Web Worker?
hints: Dedicated Worker: one-to-one with its creator tab; Shared Worker: shared across multiple tabs from the same origin
Service Worker: acts as a network proxy, has an independent lifecycle separate from any page
senior.answer: A Dedicated Worker is a background thread owned by and communicating with a single page — isolated, torn down when the page closes. A Shared Worker can be connected to by multiple pages from the same origin concurrently, enabling cross-tab state sharing (e.g., a shared WebSocket connection). A Service Worker has an entirely different lifecycle — installed independently, persists across page sessions, and acts as a network proxy intercepting fetch events. Only Service Workers can cache responses and handle push notifications.

senior.question: How does the Cache API work and how does it complement Service Workers?
hints: Cache API is a key-value store for Request/Response pairs, accessible from Service Workers and the main thread
Service Workers use it to serve cached responses when offline or to implement custom caching strategies
senior.answer: The Cache API (window.caches) is a persistent key-value store where keys are Request objects and values are Response objects. It is available in both Service Workers and the main thread. Service Workers intercept fetch events and implement caching strategies: Cache-First (serve from cache, fallback to network), Network-First (try network, fallback to cache for offline), Stale-While-Revalidate (serve cache immediately, update in background). Unlike HTTP cache, the Cache API gives full programmatic control over what is stored and when it expires.

senior.question: How do you prevent Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) in a production web application?
hints: Reserve space for all dynamic content: images (width/height attributes), ads (min-height), font swaps
Avoid inserting content above existing content; use transform for animations instead of top/left
senior.answer: CLS is caused by content shifting after initial paint. Prevention: always set explicit width and height on images and videos so the browser reserves space before they load. Reserve space for ad slots with min-height. For font swap shifts, use the size-adjust and metric-override descriptors to match fallback font dimensions. Avoid injecting banners or cookie notices above existing content after page load. Use CSS transform for animations instead of top/left/margin — transforms do not trigger layout. Measure with the Layout Instability API and monitor in RUM.

senior.question: How do you design and maintain a CI/CD pipeline for a large frontend monorepo?
hints: Use affected-module detection (Nx, Turborepo) to run only relevant tests and builds
Stages: lint → type-check → unit tests → build → E2E tests → deploy (with environment gates)
senior.answer: In a monorepo (Turborepo/Nx), affected computation runs only the tasks impacted by changed files, keeping CI fast as the repo grows. Pipeline stages: parallel lint, TypeScript type-check, and unit tests on PR; build the affected packages; run E2E tests (Cypress/Playwright) against a preview deployment; gate production deployment behind manual approval or auto-deploy on merge to main. Publish shared packages to a private registry with automated changelog generation. Cache build artifacts by hash so unchanged packages are never rebuilt.

senior.question: How do static analysis tools like ESLint and TypeScript strict mode improve code quality at scale?
hints: ESLint enforces style rules and catches common bugs; TypeScript strict mode enables noImplicitAny, strictNullChecks, etc.
Both should run in pre-commit hooks and CI to prevent regressions
senior.answer: ESLint analyses code without running it, catching potential bugs (no-floating-promises), enforcing style consistency (no-unused-vars), and applying domain-specific rules (react-hooks/rules-of-hooks, jsx-a11y). TypeScript's strict mode enables strictNullChecks (null/undefined must be handled explicitly), noImplicitAny (all types must be declared), and strictFunctionTypes (function parameter types are checked contravariantly). Run both in pre-commit hooks (lint-staged) and as required CI checks so no code reaches main with violations. Incremental adoption of strict mode uses // @ts-strict-ignore in legacy files.

senior.question: What are performance budgets in CI and how do you enforce them?
hints: Performance budgets define maximum acceptable values for bundle size, Lighthouse score, or Web Vitals
Enforce with bundlesize, Lighthouse CI, or size-limit in CI pipelines; fail builds that exceed thresholds
senior.answer: Performance budgets set numeric thresholds for metrics that directly impact user experience: maximum JS bundle size per route, minimum Lighthouse performance score, maximum LCP or TTI. Enforce them in CI using tools: size-limit checks compressed bundle sizes against a budget in package.json; Lighthouse CI runs against a preview deployment and asserts score thresholds; bundlewatch compares bundle sizes to a baseline. Failing the CI pipeline on budget violations makes performance regression visible immediately rather than after a production deploy.

senior.question: What is Atomic Design methodology and how does it apply to a component library?
hints: Atoms → Molecules → Organisms → Templates → Pages
Provides a mental model for composing increasingly complex UI from simple, reusable pieces
senior.answer: Atomic Design (Brad Frost) organises UI into five levels of complexity. Atoms are the smallest indivisible components (Button, Input, Label). Molecules combine atoms into functional units (SearchField = Input + Button). Organisms are larger, self-contained sections (Navbar = Logo + Navigation + SearchField). Templates define page layouts without real content. Pages are template instances with real content. Applied to a component library, Atoms and Molecules live in the shared design system; Organisms and above are typically product-specific, composed from the shared primitives.

senior.question: What are the deep mechanics of route-based code splitting and how do you optimise chunk granularity?
hints: React.lazy + dynamic import creates a separate chunk per route; webpack's SplitChunksPlugin controls shared chunk extraction
Too many small chunks increases HTTP overhead; too few defeats the purpose of splitting
senior.answer: Dynamic import() at route boundaries tells the bundler to emit a separate chunk per route, loaded only when that route is visited. SplitChunksPlugin (Webpack) or manualChunks (Rollup/Vite) controls how shared code is extracted into vendor or common chunks. Optimal strategy: a vendor chunk for large stable dependencies (React, lodash), per-route chunks for route-specific code, and a common chunk for code shared by 2+ routes but not all. Too-fine splitting increases HTTP round-trips (though HTTP/2 mitigates this); too-coarse splitting negates lazy loading. Profile with the bundle analyser and measure with network throttling.

senior.question: What are the key differences between a PWA and a native mobile application, and when is a PWA sufficient?
hints: PWAs run in the browser sandbox with limited hardware API access; native apps have full OS integration
PWAs deploy instantly via URL; native apps require store approval and installation
senior.answer: PWAs are web applications enhanced with Service Workers, a Web App Manifest, and HTTPS to provide offline support, home screen installation, and push notifications. They are distributed via URL without app store approval and update silently. Native apps have full OS integration (Bluetooth, NFC, advanced camera APIs, background execution), deeper system integration (widgets, Siri shortcuts), and better performance for graphics-intensive tasks. PWAs are sufficient for content, commerce, and productivity apps where the capabilities gap does not matter. Choose native when you need hardware APIs unavailable on the web or when performance-critical 60fps interaction is a non-negotiable requirement.

senior.question: How do you debug and resolve React performance problems in a large production application?
hints: Use React DevTools Profiler to identify expensive renders and their cause
Chrome Performance panel shows long tasks, layout thrashing, and JS execution time
senior.answer: Start with React DevTools Profiler: record an interaction, identify which components took longest to render and why (prop changes, context updates). The flame graph shows render time; 'why did this render' shows the trigger. Use React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback only after identifying the bottleneck — not speculatively. In Chrome Performance panel, look for long tasks (>50 ms), forced synchronous layout cycles (yellow flame-shaped bars), and large JS parse/compile costs from oversized bundles. For production RUM, inject Performance Observer to track INP and long task attribution.

senior.question: What is the React Compiler (formerly React Forget) and what problem does it solve?
hints: React Compiler automatically applies memoization by analysing component code at compile time
Eliminates the need for manual useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo in most cases
senior.answer: React Compiler (previously codenamed Forget) is a Babel transform that analyses React component and hook code at compile time and automatically inserts memoisation (equivalent to useMemo, useCallback, React.memo) where it proves safe to do so. It solves the ergonomic and correctness problems of manual memoisation: developers forget to add it (performance problem) or add it incorrectly (stale closure bugs, wasted memoisation). By making components auto-optimised, it aims to remove the need for most manual memoisation while preserving React's existing mental model. Adopted incrementally file-by-file via the opt-in directive.

senior.question: How do you design an effective end-to-end test suite for a complex SPA?
hints: Focus E2E tests on critical user journeys (checkout, auth, core workflows), not every edge case
Use Page Object Model or App Action patterns; seed test data via API, not UI interactions
senior.answer: E2E tests with Cypress or Playwright are expensive to write and maintain — invest them in critical user journeys (sign up, login, checkout, primary feature workflows) rather than exhaustive UI coverage. Use the Page Object Model or App Actions pattern to encapsulate selectors and actions, reducing maintenance when the UI changes. Set up and tear down test state via API calls or database seeding, not by navigating through the UI — this reduces test flakiness and execution time dramatically. Run E2E in CI against a deployed preview environment, parallelised across multiple workers, with automatic retry for network-flaky tests.

senior.question: How do you implement code-level feature flags and manage their lifecycle in a frontend application?
hints: Feature flags decouple deployment from release — ship code dark, enable for cohorts or percentage rollouts
Clean up flags promptly; stale flags become technical debt and introduce cognitive overhead
senior.answer: Feature flags are runtime conditionals that enable or disable functionality without redeploying. Implement them via a feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, GrowthBook) that evaluates rules server-side and streams flag values to the client. In React, a provider reads the flags and exposes them via context or hooks (useFlag('new-checkout')). Flags should have an owner, creation date, and removal ticket; unused flags must be deleted within a sprint cycle. Gate features behind flags for trunk-based development — all code merges to main; flags control who sees it.

senior.question: What is hydration in SSR/SSG React applications and what is the hydration mismatch problem?
hints: Hydration attaches React's event listeners and state to server-rendered HTML without re-rendering it
Hydration mismatch occurs when server HTML differs from what the client render would produce
senior.answer: Hydration is the process by which React takes the static HTML produced by SSR or SSG and makes it interactive — attaching event listeners, initialising state, and reconciling the client component tree with the existing DOM. If the server-rendered HTML does not match what a fresh client render would produce (due to browser-only data like window, Math.random, or Date.now), React logs a hydration mismatch warning and re-renders the affected subtree on the client, negating the performance benefit. Solutions: use useEffect for browser-only code, suppress mismatch with suppressHydrationWarning for genuinely dynamic values, or use React 18's selective hydration.

senior.question: What is the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ) in JavaScript and when does it apply?
hints: TDZ is the period between entering a block scope and the let/const declaration being initialised
Accessing a let or const variable before its declaration throws a ReferenceError
senior.answer: The Temporal Dead Zone is the region in a block scope from its entry point up to the let or const declaration. During this period, the binding exists (it is registered to the scope by the parser) but accessing it throws a ReferenceError. This contrasts with var, which is hoisted and initialised to undefined. The TDZ exists to prevent programmers from relying on variables before they are explicitly defined, enforcing cleaner, top-down code flow. Function declarations are fully hoisted and immune to TDZ.

senior.question: How would you implement a scalable permission and role-based access control system in a React SPA?
hints: Fetch permissions from the server on authentication and store them in global state or context
Guard route components and conditionally render UI elements based on permission checks
senior.answer: On login, fetch the user's roles and granular permissions from the server and store them in global state (Zustand, Redux, or Context). Create a usePermission(action, resource) hook that returns a boolean. Use it to conditionally render action buttons and UI sections. Protect routes with a PermissionGuard component that redirects unauthorised users before rendering. Crucially, always enforce permissions server-side — client-side checks are UX conveniences only; the server must re-validate every request. Represent permissions as fine-grained strings (orders:write, invoices:read) rather than coarse roles for maximum flexibility.

senior.question: How does CSS Cascade Layers work and what problem does it solve?
hints: @layer lets you define named layers with an explicit priority order, overriding specificity within layers
Solves the specificity war problem when integrating third-party CSS with custom styles
senior.answer: CSS Cascade Layers (@layer) allow authors to define named groups of styles with an explicit priority order declared upfront: @layer base, components, utilities. Rules in a higher-priority layer override those in lower layers regardless of selector specificity within those layers. This solves the CSS specificity escalation problem when mixing third-party library styles with custom code — instead of out-specificity-ing library selectors with increasingly complex chains, you simply place your overrides in a higher layer.

senior.question: How do you architect multi-tenant frontend applications where each tenant has custom branding and features?
hints: Resolve tenant at the edge or SSR from the hostname/subdomain; inject design tokens and feature flags per tenant
Store per-tenant config server-side; never trust client-supplied tenant IDs for authorisation
senior.answer: Identify the tenant from the hostname or subdomain at the edge or in SSR middleware. Fetch that tenant's configuration (design tokens, enabled features, logo, custom copy) from a configuration API. Inject design tokens as CSS custom properties so the component library is unstyled at the base and themed per tenant without code changes. Use feature flags to enable tenant-specific features. Serve tenant-specific assets from a CDN path keyed by tenant ID. Never derive authorisation or data scoping from client-supplied tenant identifiers — always verify from the authenticated session server-side.

senior.question: What are the trade-offs between CSS-in-JS libraries (styled-components, Emotion) and build-time styling approaches (CSS Modules, Tailwind)?
hints: CSS-in-JS enables dynamic styling from JS values and co-location with components but adds runtime overhead
Build-time approaches produce static CSS with zero runtime cost but require different patterns for dynamic styles
senior.answer: CSS-in-JS libraries (styled-components, Emotion) co-locate styles with component code, support dynamic styling from props or theme values, and eliminate dead CSS automatically. The cost: a runtime that parses template literals, generates class names, and injects stylesheets — adding KB to the JS bundle and CPU work on every render. In React Server Components they require special configuration. Build-time approaches (CSS Modules, Tailwind, vanilla-extract) generate all CSS at compile time with zero runtime overhead and smaller bundles. Dynamic styles must use CSS custom properties or class toggling rather than arbitrary JS expressions. The trend is toward build-time solutions for performance-sensitive applications.

senior.question: How do you handle frontend observability — logging, error tracking, and performance monitoring — in a production SPA?
hints: Integrate an error tracking service (Sentry) to capture and group unhandled exceptions with stack traces
Use Real User Monitoring (RUM) for performance (Web Vitals, long tasks); structured logging for diagnostics
senior.answer: Error tracking: integrate Sentry or Datadog RUM to automatically capture unhandled exceptions and promise rejections with source-mapped stack traces, breadcrumbs of user actions, and session replay. Performance monitoring: instrument Core Web Vitals with the web-vitals library and ship data to an analytics endpoint or RUM service. Structured logging: use a consistent log schema (severity, correlation ID, user ID) and route logs to a centralised aggregator. Avoid logging PII. Set up alerting on error rate spikes and P75/P95 LCP/INP regressions. Correlate frontend errors with backend trace IDs by passing a request ID through the fetch header.

senior.question: How do you manage and version shared component library releases to avoid breaking downstream consumers?
hints: Use semantic versioning (semver); breaking changes require a major version bump and a migration guide
Automate changelog generation and release notes from conventional commit messages with tools like semantic-release
senior.answer: Publish the component library as a versioned npm package following semver: patch for bug fixes, minor for backward-compatible additions, major for breaking changes. Use conventional commits (feat, fix, BREAKING CHANGE) with a tool like semantic-release or changesets to automate version bumping and changelog generation on merge to main. Maintain a clear migration guide for major versions. Communicate upcoming breaking changes via deprecation warnings in the current major version before removing in the next. Consider a canary release channel for teams to validate changes before the stable release.
